speaker: interviewer
text: Jacob, we just got to the point where you found that your brother, Andrew, was alive in [NON-ENGLISH]. And that was 3,000 kilometers away in the Ural mountains?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Past the Ural mountains.

speaker: interviewer
text: Past the Ural mountains. How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I get there by train. Three days it take me to go to--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened when you got there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, I find him healthy, all right. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: He'd been there during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: No?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Oh, yeah, he has. He had been there. But he had been there-- when he left Lwow, he-- yes, he had been there from '42, I suppose. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you take him anything? Did you take anything to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I take apples. [LAUGHS] Because in the Ukraine, the apple was-- and they have got in [NON-ENGLISH]. It was cold. I carry a bag with apples, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did he come back with you? Did he come back with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. He was in the organization, too. And when they started to-- they make a list to come over, and they organized the carriages to come back. And he come back later on. I was earlier, because I was nearer. And I was early, and I went to [NON-ENGLISH]. And I organized for him, when he come back, a house-- not a house, yes, a flat to live. And what he needs, everything to have a baby. And I prepared everything, what they need. Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go back to Kiev first? Did you go back to Kiev?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I went back to Kiev, yes. Because my nephew dies.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And were you in Moscow, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd been in Moscow two years. I'd been in Moscow, because the organization has the main quarters in Moscow, of the [NON-ENGLISH] in Moscow. So I've been often in Moscow, and I'd been in Moscow, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then did you go back to Poland and back to Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. When it come the time, they let me know I have to go to the railway station and [NON-ENGLISH] back to Poland, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when you go back to Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not Warsaw, back to Stettin.

speaker: interviewer
text: But did you through--

speaker: interviewee
text: I go to Warsaw, yes. I've gone to Warsaw a few times, because we always have some business in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find out-- did you find out what happened to the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, certainly not. I think nobody knows anything. And just was the ghetto and was the fight in the ghetto, and people were taken from the ghetto, and this was all that you could know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even my cousin, who was-- the cousin who passed away last year appear. And he was outside the ghetto all the war, outside the ghetto in Warsaw. And he didn't know how this has happened, what happened. So who can know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he told me he used to even bring for the ghetto some goods through the-- through the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Fence.

speaker: interviewee
text: Fence, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said you went to Stettin. Was that Poland or Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was German before the war, and it was Poland after the war. Because the [NON-ENGLISH]. The--

speaker: interviewer
text: Border?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oder was the border.

speaker: interviewer
text: The river Oder.

speaker: interviewee
text: The river Oder. Yeah. It was the border between Germany and Poland. And Stettin was-- there was Stettin, [NON-ENGLISH], all this belongs to Poland, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you go to that town?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. This was-- everybody was going, because this is a not destroyed town. And it is easier to find work, and everybody was going. So I went, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do when you got there?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I got there, I was working-- working for an organization. I have-- who has some-- has some farms. Yes. They have-- they have 24 farms. 24 farms, with 12 farms in [NON-ENGLISH] and 12 farms in [NON-ENGLISH]. And I was the-- the-- the top agronomist for the farms. And I have to very hard work, because was everything not organized. And the-- everybody was corrupt, very corrupt, in the farms. And I have to organize the farms to look after the-- not to destroy the-- the-- the livestock and to keep everything in order, and to produce everything that is needed, not to destroy. And this was a very difficult job, because the Poles were sitting on the farms. They were very corrupt.

speaker: interviewer
text: They take just-- and they sell on the side. And that. And how long did you stay in Stettin?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Stettin? I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] From Stettin we come to Australia. So it's all time. I think it is eight years or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And did you marry again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. I met my wife, yes. And you want to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, we'll see her photo, yes. How did you meet her?

speaker: interviewee
text: I meet her in an organization where we worked together, and she was in the office. And so we meet there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she's not Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Halina. Halina, yes. [NON-ENGLISH] was her name, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she survived the war in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: She survived the war in Poland, yeah. She was originally from Poznan. Yes, she survived the war, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I have two children-- Peter, my son, and Helen, my daughter. And they come-- we're all together come to Australia, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And how did you-- why did you decide to emigrate to Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because this was a time when they-- when they-- when they take the all Jews from the posts. They saw it was not-- it's belonging there. And it was not a good atmosphere for Jews. There always was anti-Semitism was very strong. And my wife, she was Polish. And she was very young. When she heard when Peter was on the street. And the boys called him [NON-ENGLISH]. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: The same as before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, but even stronger among the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Under the communists?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did you have any incidents yourself? At work or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not incidents. But you feel as this is not what you--

speaker: interviewer
text: And why did you migrate to Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: I migrate to Australia. I have the possibilities to go to Canada. And I find out that I have a cousin up here in Sydney. So I was in touch with him. I write him a letter, and I ask him if he want to be a sponsor for me to come to Australia. So we decided to go to Australia, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you come?

speaker: interviewer
text: 1958, yes. And could you speak English when you came?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not a word. No, absolutely not. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you learn?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, in a very, very hard way. The same way I learn Russian. I learn German. I learned because you have to when you have to work, and you have to-- you just pick up and learn. And this is the way. I haven't got the possibility to go study, because I work at the time. I have to work all the time. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you find work when you came to Sydney?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not easy to find work in my line, because I wouldn't go-- want to up here on a farm. Because this is-- and when I want to work for some nursery man, he told me he don't need a specialist. He's a specialist himself. He need some people to work, not a specialist.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I start to work, because I want to know the conditions up here, the circumstances, the climate and everything what-- so I start to work in a nursery. I work in nursery, [INAUDIBLE]. And yes. And then later? What did you do later?

speaker: interviewee
text: And at the same time, I start to build up my own nursery. So I have the possibilities to take some cuttings, what I need from the nursery. He just allowed me to take-- yes, was a nice fellow, Australian. What you need, you can have it. [SOB]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was working and building up a nursery. And when comes the time, I was working by my cousin. He runs a company, a plastic company. And she was working there. So when I was ready with the nursery, I start to work myself. And who-- who was your cousin? Would you like to say his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Henry Perry, but this is a new name, Perry. The real name is Pernikasz. This is a name that no English person can spell or can say. So he changed from Pernikasz to Perry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he come before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. He came after the war, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he the only survivor from your family? Apart from your two brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. He has a sister who survived, and she was in Tel Aviv, but she passed away a time ago. She was up here-- oh, yes, she was visiting. And I think this is the only one, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had-- your children came. They were already in school when they came to Australia? They went to school here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They went to school here, yes. They went to school. To primary school, and later to high school, and to university. And my daughter was in college, and Peter was in university. And he studied electricity, and the daughter studied chemistry.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I have two grandchildren. Peter has two children. I have two grandchildren. And Helen's not-- was not married. She hasn't got children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And are you in any way connected with the Jewish community here in Sydney?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- not-- not with the organized Jewish community. I am connected with some Jews that I know, they are friends. But not in an organized manner, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. And how would you describe your beliefs after all you went through in the war? Would you say you were religious or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I am not religious. I've never been religious. I'm not religious. And-- but I'm Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Why do you think-- why do you think you survived? Do you think it was luck or-- why do you think you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody knows. Was it luck? [INAUDIBLE] luck and-- I don't know. Nobody knows [LAUGHS] what--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did you ever talk to your children or your grandchildren about your story? About your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not [INAUDIBLE]. I never talked with the grandchildren about the story. They are not much interested, and this is a very sad story. This wasn't good for young children to tell them. Maybe later on, when they are older, and then my son can tell them, or my daughter will tell them, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't want them to see the video now? At their age, now that they're teenagers?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I think Peter will decide on whether to show them or not. I don't know.

words.text: whether to show them or not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe later.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe later, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about with your son and your daughter? Did you talk about--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I told him. Of course, yes. But not so exact. I don't think that they remember much from what I talk, but it's not easy to talk about, these things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did you ever receive any compensation or restitution from Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was a fellow, a solicitor, a Jewish solicitor. But he's not alive yet. And he was-- what is his name? I don't remember his name. I couldn't remember now his name. And he received some few hundred dollars. Yes, some 20 years ago or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. In Australia. You received that in Australia? Yes. And did you ever receive anything to say you were in the Polish army? Did they acknowledge your being in the Army, from Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, did you have medals or decorations or anything like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, I have a few medals from the partisans, a medal from the partisans and from the army. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [NON-ENGLISH] and a medal, yes, from the Polish government.

speaker: interviewer
text: No pension or anything like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. Have you been back to Poland since you came to Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I've been in Poland with my wife. I've been in Poland, yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you feel when you go back there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not special. No. Because I just-- I've been in Poland. I rent a car, and I went around to Gdansk, just to visit different places, just for fun. Nothing special. Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go back to Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And have you been to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I've been in Israel in '57. And I've been in Israel eight years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you feel when you're in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, very good. I have a lot of friends, and my brother, he take me everywhere. To Jerusalem, and to Bethlehem. And then I went-- from Israel, I went to Egypt for a few days. I've been in [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And would you say you've had a good life in Australia? Has it been--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's been good for me, yes, but it was not easy. I was working very hard. I was working 16, 18 hours a day when I start my nursery, because I was working outside and in the late night, I was working in my glass houses and push houses. It was not easy. And I have a place in the [INAUDIBLE] markets. I have a store, and I have to go in 4 o'clock in the morning to go up to take some plants to the market. And it was not easy, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're retired now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I'm retired. I was retired now. Eight years, I'm retired.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And you said your wife passed away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have any health problems, do you think, from your war time experiences? Do you feel it affected your health in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. Of course it affected my health. Yes, I had some-- I don't want to talk about the problems what I have. Yes, I have some problems. I have two operations done. A prostate operation and a hernia operation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Thank you. Thank you for telling your story.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you, thank you. Yes. This is my mother, my father. They were married in Warsaw. This is a photo of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what year that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do know what year that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture we find after the war in Warsaw, my uncle. And this is I and my brother, Andrew. And this, I think we have five or six years.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Warsaw, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: There are pictures after the war. And one was early, when I-- without the glasses. I haven't got any glasses after the war. And later on, this is the second picture that I wear glasses.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture made in Szczecin after the war. And this is my wife and Peter and me. And this is outside Szczecin, close to [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is Helen and Peter, when they were little.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Szczecin?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Szczecin, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is my son, Peter, and Helen. This is when they start to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is a group before we went from Germany, just my brother with his wife. And I with the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is it Andrew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Andrew, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's his wife's name, and the child?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Oh, boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Don't worry. It doesn't matter. This is in Szczecin, before you left Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Germany. OK. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is was the picture taken in Szczecin in 1956, I suppose. That's my wife, and Helen, and me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is before we left from Szczecin. This is close to the harbor, where we take the ship. The boat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Say that again?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is from the boat, on the boat. [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Coming to Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Coming to Australia, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is Moshe, my brother. In Tel Aviv.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a few medals, what I received from the Polish government and from the partisans-- [NON-ENGLISH] they call, for partisans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you say what the small ones are at the bottom?

speaker: interviewee
text: This [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And this one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, [POINTS] this is what I say. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this one is from 10 years? This was 1954.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a university medal. A university medal from the Polish government.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: There, the passport that I received from the German government was called [NON-ENGLISH]. Means people who hasn't got a--

speaker: interviewer
text: A country.

speaker: interviewee
text: A country.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is a picture taken in Blacktown, from when I received the Australian citizenship from the mayor of Blacktown.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the date, when that was? 1960? Sometime in the 1960s?

speaker: interviewee
text: Something like that, yeah. 1960s.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is my wife, Halina, before we married in Szczecin.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is a picture in Blacktown, when I start my own business. I built a nursery, and this is a glass house built from scratch.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture in Paris, in holiday with my wife and daughter, on a ship on the Rhine.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the Seine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Seine, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this-- this is a group in Sydney [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] before a restaurant. And say their names.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It's Peter and Helen, and Amy and Anthony, my grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is Peter and Helen now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your two children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two children, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is Peter's graduation from university. A picture of my wife and me. And this is Helen and my wife and me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is a group. Peter, with his children, and my grandchildren. And this is Amy, Anthony, and Peter together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is a picture of Andrew's daughter, Elise, in America. And her family, three boys and a husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where do they live?

speaker: interviewee
text: They live in Virginia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. I like to draw, and I like to paint. And this is one of my drawings, made from a little-- a very little photo in Poland. This is a little church in the mountains.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: A year ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is--  [LAUGHS] when I find out I can draw, I started to draw myself, and I take a mirror, and I said, sit down and draw myself. And [INAUDIBLE] not bad.

words.text: is--  [LAUGHS] when I find out I can draw,

speaker: interviewer
text: Is it fairly recent? You did that recently?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. A year ago, maybe, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, it's very good. Thank you, Jacob.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
